title: Feasibility of Wrist-Worn, Cancelable, Real-Time Biometric Authentication via HD-sEMG and Dynamic Gestures
venue: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement

method.training_strategy: Cross-entropy loss with 60/20/20 split for training, validation, and testing

evaluation.context: Achieved in the most challenging situation (Situation 2: imposters perform correct gesture code) with a code length of 12
Achieved during real-time biometric evaluation via wristband prototype
Achieved during real-time biometric evaluation via wristband prototype

evaluation_protocol: Three scenarios: Situation 1 (imposters unknown code), Situation 2 (imposters known code), Situation 3 (imposters use user's biometric traits). Evaluated across increasing code lengths (L=1 to 12).

reported_limitations: Performance degradation due to between-day electrode shifts and user adaptation

usability.usability_issues: Long code lengths increase authentication time and risk of gesture code exposure

key_contribution: Verified feasibility of wrist-worn HD-sEMG for cancelable authentication using only 16 channels
Implemented a real-time authentication system using dynamic gestures to reduce latency
Demonstrated the use of cancelable codes to protect against biometric trait leakage
main_strength: Very low EER even when imposters know the gesture code
Low computational latency for real-time use
main_weakness: Significant performance drop between different days
Lack of testing for various arm postures
insights.notes_for_review: The paper emphasizes the balance between security (code length) and usability (authentication speed).
